headline: What Would Steve Jobs Do Today?
summary: Steve Jobs, the co-founder, long-time CEO of Apple (NASDAQ: AAPL), and the heart and soul of the company, died in 2011. If he were alive, he would watch his handpicked CEO, Tim Cook, retire. Cook did almost everything right for Apple’s shareholders. But he did little to change the company’s products. The iPhone was first ... What Would Steve Jobs Do Today?

headline: Apple's Rally: Pricing Power, AI Discipline, And The Memory Crunch
summary: Apple (AAPL) stock dropped 6% on June 25 after the company raised Mac and iPad prices by $100 to $300, blaming a memory shortage CEO Tim Cook called a "hundred-year flood." Less than two weeks later, shares have clawed back most of that loss, trading near 52-week highs. The market appears to be recalculating Apple's position on two fronts: how well placed Apple is inside the DRAM shortage relative to its peers and how its restraint on AI spending looks increasingly like a smart move.

raw.headline: Apple's Rally: Pricing Power, AI Discipline, And The Memory Crunch
raw.summary: Apple (AAPL) stock dropped 6% on June 25 after the company raised Mac and iPad prices by $100 to $300, blaming a memory shortage CEO Tim Cook called a "hundred-year flood." Less than two weeks later, shares have clawed back most of that loss, trading near 52-week highs. The market appears to be recalculating Apple's position on two fronts: how well placed Apple is inside the DRAM shortage relative to its peers and how its restraint on AI spending looks increasingly like a smart move.

headline: Broadcom, Apple extend chip supply deal through 2031
summary: Broadcom said on Monday it has agreed to expand its partnership with Apple through 2031 to develop and supply ‌custom chips, easing concerns over the iPhone maker's reliance on the ‌chipmaker.  The chipmaker, whose shares jumped nearly 4% in premarket trading, has been supplying key components to ​Apple for a very long time, including radio frequency chips used in iPhones for connecting to cellular networks, Wi-Fi and Bluetooth connectivity chips and other networking semiconductors.  Despite developing its own chips, including the C1 modem, Apple relies on Broadcom for wireless and radio-frequency components.

raw.headline: Broadcom, Apple extend chip supply deal through 2031
raw.summary: Broadcom said on Monday it has agreed to expand its partnership with Apple through 2031 to develop and supply ‌custom chips, easing concerns over the iPhone maker's reliance on the ‌chipmaker.  The chipmaker, whose shares jumped nearly 4% in premarket trading, has been supplying key components to ​Apple for a very long time, including radio frequency chips used in iPhones for connecting to cellular networks, Wi-Fi and Bluetooth connectivity chips and other networking semiconductors.  Despite developing its own chips, including the C1 modem, Apple relies on Broadcom for wireless and radio-frequency components.
